phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: When generating clinical output, model the seven qualities of bare attention: impartial, open, nonjudgmental, interested, patient, fearless, and impersonal. Output language should reflect evenly suspended attention—withholding advice-giving, reactive analysis, or agenda-driven interpretation in favor of neutral witnessing language.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when composing any client-facing language or clinical recommendations. The Mechanic's output quality should model the attentional stance the clinician should embody.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Additive: The seven qualities of bare attention are the phenomenological description of what it feels like to be in a Ventral Vagal state. M9 provides autonomic mechanism (ventral vagal activation, social engagement system online) that enables the attentional stance M22 describes. If social engagement system is offline (no prosody, flat voice), bare attention cannot be authentically accessed.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client uses language of 'emptiness,' 'unworthiness,' 'insatiable longing,' 'nothing fills me up,' or describes patterns of addiction, low self-esteem, and futile grasping to fill inner lack, then flag as Hungry Ghost realm / basic fault activation. Withhold celebration or progress framing. Use neutral acknowledgment. Route to psychotherapy-first track. Do not deepen contemplative work.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered during intake review or when client language patterns suggest core wound of absence from inadequate early attention. Client is searching for gratification for old needs whose time has passed.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Confluent: M22 basic fault and M16 Exile are the same clinical phenomenon. Both reference preverbal developmental wounds creating core emptiness/unworthiness. M22's warning that meditation can surface basic fault without resolving it parallels M16's warning that bypassing Protectors causes backlash. Check for Exile carrying unworthiness burden.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If intake reveals Western cultural background AND presents with feelings of alienation, emptiness, or basic fault indicators (Hungry Ghost markers) AND no established psychotherapy addressing core developmental wound, then psychotherapy must come first or run parallel before introducing formal meditation practice. Use concentration techniques only within session for stabilization. Do not prescribe home meditation practice.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered at intake assessment when determining treatment sequencing. Western estrangement pattern means client lacks the 'good enough' early attention that creates secure base. Meditation before psychotherapy creates clinical danger—surfaces wound without healing capacity.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Additive: Western estrangement (basic fault from inadequate holding) maps to Winnicott's holding environment failure. Confirm holding environment present via M21 assessment before meditation is safe. Establish relational holding and address basic fault before deepening contemplative work.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client reports extensive meditation practice AND continues to exhibit Hungry Ghost patterns (emptiness, insatiable longing, unworthiness) AND shows no behavioral change or improved relationships despite meditation, then flag as defensive meditation / spiritual bypass. Redirect to psychotherapy to address Exile/basic fault. Hold meditation practice at concentration level only.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when reviewing client history showing meditation practice alongside persistent Hungry Ghost patterns. Manager can perform meditation as sophisticated control strategy to keep Exiles suppressed. True test: does practice lead to behavioral change and relational improvement, or just more sophisticated avoidance?
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Confluent: M22 defensive meditation and M16 Manager-performing-Self are identical phenomena. Both describe sophisticated defense where client appears to be doing the work while actually reinforcing suppression of core wounds. Use Feel Towards test (M16) to distinguish genuine Self-energy from Manager performing Self.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If when prompted to observe internal sensation, client immediately analyzes ('I think it's because...'), judges ('This is bad'), or avoids (changes subject, fidgets) AND cannot sustain non-reactive awareness for even brief moments, then client lacks bare attention capacity (observing ego not developed). Focus on cultivating observing ego through concentration practice (samadhi) within session only. Do not prescribe mindfulness (vipassana) practice.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered during session when client cannot separate reactions from raw sensory events. Client is 'swept away' by experience—needs capacity to witness before capacity to investigate. Observing ego capacity is prerequisite for all advanced work.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Confluent: M22 observing ego and M16 Self-energy describe the same psychological capacity. Observing ego is the faculty that can witness mental/emotional processes without blending; Self-energy is the emergent quality when parts unblend. If client cannot hold Self-presence, they cannot hold bare attention.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client reports meditation experiences of 'seeing dissolution,' 'feeling foundations crumble,' 'seeing lions/tigers/leopards,' or other imagery of threat/precariousness AND shows dorsal markers (flat voice, numb/foggy, disconnection) OR sympathetic FEAR markers (darting eyes, freezing, jumpiness), then immediately halt deepening of meditation practice. This is autonomic dysregulation, not spiritual breakthrough. Intervention is state restoration (Track 01 Settle) and co-regulation, not deeper practice.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when client reports intense meditation experiences accompanied by autonomic dysregulation markers. Terror state indicates meditation has exceeded client's capacity for safe ego deconstruction. Must restore regulation and address basic fault before proceeding.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Extends: M22 Terror state maps to the edge of Window of Tolerance where ventral vagal regulation begins to dissolve into sympathetic or dorsal activation. M9 provides mechanism: client has been pushed to edge where ventral regulation dissolves. Without adequate holding, meditation surfaces dissolution that client's nervous system cannot tolerate.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction:
If client consistently uses spatial language to describe self: 'finding my core,' 'protecting my boundaries,' 'getting to the bottom of things,' 'I have layers,' 'discovering the real me,' then client is in spatial self-metaphor stage. Parts work (M16) is appropriate. Temporal/processual language is premature — do not force it. Use spatial language in interventions.

RESOLUTION (M22_CROSS_06): Spatial self-metaphor is not error; it is developmentally necessary scaffolding. M16 parts framework operates correctly within spatial metaphor and is the appropriate primary framework for Phases 1–4. The M22 progression beyond spatial metaphor applies only when client demonstrates: (1) stable Self-leadership, (2) most Exiles unburdened, (3) spontaneous curiosity about impermanence, (4) ventral regulation without clinging to permanent states. Until all four are present, maintain M16 spatial framework. Do not introduce M22 temporal reframe.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when assessing client's self-concept language patterns. Spatial metaphor is developmentally necessary for many Western clients with basic fault. Forcing temporal deconstruction prematurely destabilizes necessary achievements.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Conflicts with resolution: M22 presents spatial metaphor as preliminary stage before temporal metaphor; M16 parts model is fundamentally spatial. Resolution: Use parts language (M16 spatial) when client needs structure and coherence (Phases 1-4, basic fault active). Introduce temporal language only when client shows readiness: stable Self-leadership, most Exiles unburdened, spontaneous curiosity about impermanence.
phase6_rules.conflict_flags: RESOLVED (M22_CROSS_06): M16_IFS spatial parts model and M22 spatial-to-temporal progression are sequential, not competing. M16 spatial framework is correct for Phases 1–4. M22 temporal progression applies only at Phase 5–6 with four readiness indicators confirmed. Conflict closes when sequencing gate (M22_AGENT_08) governs the transition.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction:
If client spontaneously uses temporal language: 'I'm a process,' 'I'm always changing,' 'I'm learning to ride the waves,' 'I'm not the same person I was yesterday' AND shows comfort with impermanence AND demonstrates stable Self-leadership across states, then client is ready for temporal self-metaphor framework. Can introduce mindfulness (vipassana) practice that observes flux. Can frame goals as processual rather than fixed-state achievements.

RESOLUTION (M22_CROSS_06): Temporal self-metaphor readiness does not retire M16 parts framework — parts language remains available alongside temporal reframes. When introducing temporal perspective, do not invalidate parts work already done. Frame as: parts understanding remains accurate AND self is also a flowing process. These are complementary lenses at Phase 5–6, not a replacement of M16 by M22. If client shows instability when temporal framing is introduced, revert to M16 spatial framework — dissolution cascade indicates readiness criteria were not met.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when client language and presentation indicate developmental readiness for temporal view of self. Temporal metaphor represents progression beyond spatial view, allowing greater resilience and flexibility.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Modifier: Temporal metaphor may conflict with parts model. Assess whether client can hold both: parts as useful map while recognizing processual nature. M9 applies: Temporal metaphor requires wide Window of Tolerance.
phase6_rules.conflict_flags: RESOLVED (M22_CROSS_06): M16 parts framework and M22 temporal view are not mutually exclusive at Phase 5–6. Resolution: hold both lenses simultaneously. M16 explains the content of experience (these parts, these patterns); M22 explains the nature of the experiencer (process, not entity). Introduce temporal view as additional layer, never as replacement. Monitor for dissolution instability.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client expresses 'I know why I do this but I can't stop' AND has intellectual insight into patterns ('remembering') but continues to enact them compulsively ('repeating') AND shows frustration with lack of change despite therapy, then client is stuck in repeating phase between remembering and working-through. Stop insight-focused work. Use Feel Towards test (M16) to identify concerned Protector blocking change. Get to know Protector's fears about change. Obtain explicit permission before returning to Exile/basic-fault work.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when therapy achieves remembering but lacks mechanism for working-through. Repeating occurs when therapy touches Exile without Protector permission.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Confluent: M22 repeating phase and M16 Protector backlash describe the same clinical stalemate. Both indicate need to return to Protector negotiation before proceeding. M22 provides Freudian framework; M16 provides parts-specific intervention.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client demonstrates: (1) ability to stay with difficult material using mindfulness instead of acting it out, (2) becoming more 'conversant with resistance' and changing view of it, (3) small but consistent behavioral changes integrating past insights, (4) uses awareness to notice and interrupt old patterns in moment, then client is in working-through phase (Phases 3-5). Support and deepen use of mindfulness and surrender. Can advance meditation practice if autonomic capacity supports it.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when assessing client progress beyond mere insight. Working-through is the long process of integrating unconscious material, becoming conversant with resistance. Therapist patience is key component.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Confluent: M22 working-through and M18 CI inquiry are the same process: sustained engagement with difficult material to integrate awareness into behavior. CI provides micro-intervention tools (Stepping Stones) for each moment of M22's long-arc working-through process.

phase6_rules.rule_name: Strong Ego Prerequisite for Insight Practice
phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client requests or is being considered for advanced meditation (insight/vipassana-nana stage that deconstructs self-concept) BUT does not demonstrate: (1) capacity to witness difficult material without blending, (2) Protectors give permission without qualified yes, (3) can tolerate intense emotion without Firefighter activation, (4) wide Window of Tolerance with capacity to traverse sympathetic and return to ventral safely, then client lacks strong ego / Self-leadership prerequisite for advanced meditation. Do not proceed with insight practice. Continue building Self-leadership through concentration practice and Self-Protector relationship work.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when considering advanced meditation for client. Strong ego is NOT False Self control—it's observing ego resilient enough to witness dissolution without collapse. Without strong ego, deconstruction becomes fragmentation.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Confluent: M22 'strong ego' requirement maps directly to M16 Self-leadership capacity. Both frameworks agree: deconstructive work requires stable witness capacity. Use Feel Towards test and Protector permission assessment. M9 applies: Strong ego requires wide Window of Tolerance.

phase6_rules.rule_name: Bare Attention Clinician Stance During High Reactivity
phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client shows high reactivity (intense emotional activation, rapid state shifts, flooding) AND verbal interventions increase dysregulation rather than support regulation, then clinician shifts to explicit bare attention stance: maintain evenly suspended attention, withhold verbal intervention, model non-reactive presence. Clinician's own bare attention becomes the holding intervention. Can explicitly name the attentional quality being offered: 'I'm going to stay present with you without trying to fix or change what's happening.'
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when client is flooded and words increase dysregulation. Clinician's non-reactive presence is more regulating than words. Modeling bare attention teaches client the stance through direct transmission.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Additive: Bare attention IS a form of holding—emotional poise without impingement. M9 applies: Bare attention requires clinician in ventral state—cannot model this while blended with own Manager. M16 applies: Clinician must be in Self-energy to hold bare attention stance.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction:
If client expresses goal to 'find my True Self' OR 'discover the real me' OR frames therapy as search for fixed authentic identity AND shows attachment to this as permanent state to achieve, then:

PHASES 1–4: Use M21 Winnicott framing. Support True Self work as recovery of spontaneous gesture over False Self compliance. This is therapeutically necessary — do not introduce M22 caution prematurely. Client needs coherent self before deconstructing self-concept.

PHASE 5–6 ONLY, with readiness indicators confirmed: Introduce Epstein's reframe — 'What if there's no fixed True Self to find, just authentic responding in each moment?' Reframe goal from finding a thing (spatial) to cultivating a capacity (temporal).

RESOLUTION (M22_CROSS_10): The M21/M22 conflict on True Self is a sequencing conflict, not a doctrinal incompatibility. M21 True Self recovery is the necessary developmental precondition for M22 True Self deconstruction. You cannot deconstruct what has not first been recovered. Default to M21 unless all four readiness indicators are confirmed: (1) stable authentic relating without rigid identity dependence, (2) comfort with impermanence, (3) ventral regulation without permanent-state clinging, (4) explicit client interest in Buddhist perspective.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when client presents True Self as fixed goal to achieve. M21 uses True Self as therapeutic endpoint; M22 warns this creates new trap. Navigate tension based on client developmental stage.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Conflicts with resolution: M21 treats True Self as goal; M22 treats it as potential trap. Resolution: Use developmental sequencing—M21 True Self work appropriate for Phases 1-4 (building coherent self). M22 deconstruction appropriate only for Phase 6 clients who demonstrate readiness and explicit interest in Buddhist practice.
phase6_rules.conflict_flags: RESOLVED (M22_CROSS_10): M21 True Self as endpoint and M22 True Self as attachment are teleologically incompatible but sequentially compatible. M21 recovery of spontaneous gesture is Phase 1–4 work and is the developmental prerequisite for M22 deconstruction. M22 deconstruction without prior M21 recovery is clinically dangerous — destabilizes coherence before it is established. Resolution rule: M21 governs until all four Phase 5–6 readiness indicators are met. M22 then extends M21, it does not replace it.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If considering prescribing meditation homework BUT client shows: (1) unstable ventral regulation (cannot maintain Green state without co-regulation), (2) no established relational holding (M21), (3) active Firefighter behaviors (addiction, self-harm, dissociation), (4) basic fault surfacing without therapy addressing it, OR (5) Protectors show qualified permission or concerns, then do NOT prescribe meditation homework. Use concentration techniques within session only for stabilization. Establish co-regulation capacity, relational holding, and address Firefighter concerns before assigning home practice.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when considering homework prescription. Meditation becomes defensive bypass under these conditions. Without adequate holding and regulation capacity, home practice reinforces avoidance or causes overwhelm.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Gate: Requires stable ventral baseline (M9). Requires holding environment established (M21). Requires Protector permission without qualified yes (M16). Can use brief Stone 6 embodiment in session (M18) as alternative to formal meditation.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client demonstrates: (1) stable Green/ventral state within session, (2) capacity for sustained focus on single object (breath, sensation) without flooding or dissociation, (3) observing ego showing seven qualities of bare attention, (4) can witness thoughts/sensations without immediate reaction, then client is ready for progression from concentration (samadhi) to mindfulness (vipassana) practice. Can introduce meditation that shifts attention across succession of objects to observe flux. Can begin temporal metaphor language.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when assessing readiness for meditation progression. Concentration creates tranquility and observing ego. Mindfulness observes flux and introduces temporal view. Cannot skip concentration stage—observing ego must be functional first.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Additive: Concentration requires ventral regulation; mindfulness requires capacity to traverse sympathetic safely (wide Window of Tolerance). M16 applies: Observing ego capacity parallels Self-leadership—must be able to witness parts without blending.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client intellectualizes, analyzes, or dissociates into story/narrative when discussing experience AND is disconnected from present embodied sensation, then use Stone 6 (M18) as bridge to Epstein's 'remembering the present': 'That's analysis. What do you actually FEEL in your body right now?' Anchor client in present-moment sensation. If client cannot access present sensation (tuning out, dissociation), this indicates basic fault activation requiring co-regulation before inquiry can proceed.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when client disconnects from present sensation into narrative or analysis. Buddhist remembering is returning attention to present-moment experience. Stone 6 operationalizes this. Both meditation and CI require embodied presence prerequisite.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Confluent: Stone 6 is the clinical technique for Epstein's 'remembering the present.' M9 applies: Present-moment awareness requires ventral state. M21 applies: Capacity for present-moment experience requires holding environment.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client presents Hungry Ghost pattern (insatiable longing, emptiness, unworthiness) AND inquiry surfaces that pattern originates in developmental moment where child learned to ignore gut/True Self to maintain caregiver relationship, then use integrated M22/M18 framework for client education: 'You're not broken—you're searching for something that was missing early on' (M22 Hungry Ghost destigmatization). 'You learned to ignore what you needed to keep the relationship' (M18 attachment-wound mechanism). Use Stones 10-11 to access moment attachment was chosen over authenticity.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when origin tracing reveals attachment-authenticity split underlying Hungry Ghost presentation. Both frameworks agree: healing requires addressing developmental wound, not managing symptoms.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Extends: M22 Hungry Ghost and M18 attachment-wound describe same developmental injury from complementary angles. Hungry Ghost is felt experience (insatiable longing); attachment wound is etiology. M21 applies: Hungry Ghost indicates holding environment failure. M16 applies: Hungry Ghost pattern maintained by Exile carrying unworthiness burden.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If asked to observe part from Self-distance (M16 unblending) OR asked to hold bare attention toward internal experience, client shows: (1) inability to tolerate ambiguous intermediate zone where experience is neither 'me' nor 'not-me', (2) needs rigid boundaries, (3) cannot play with perspective, (4) shows False Self compliance when asked to observe, then client lacks transitional space capacity—prerequisite for both meditation (M22) and parts work (M16). Focus on building holding environment (M21) and ventral regulation (M9) before proceeding with contemplative practice or deep parts exploration.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when client cannot tolerate the intermediate zone required for witnessing. Transitional space capacity is foundational for both meditation and parts work. Without it, both interventions cause fragmentation or False Self compliance.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Extends: M22 applies Winnicott's transitional space to meditation—bare attention creates psychological space where self can be questioned without threat. M16 unblending occurs within this space. M21 holding environment is prerequisite for developing transitional space capacity. M9 applies: Transitional space requires ventral state baseline.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If client has achieved insight via CI inquiry (Stone 13 identifies core belief, Stones 8-9 trace origin) BUT needs sustained mechanism for integrating awareness into behavioral change (stuck in repeating, not yet working-through), then prescribe bare attention meditation as homework for working-through. Only prescribe if client has: (1) ventral regulation capacity, (2) basic relational holding established, (3) no active Firefighter behaviors. Otherwise default to in-session Stone 6 embodiment as working-through tool.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when CI has surfaced material but integration mechanism needed. Psychotherapy provides micro-interventions to surface material; meditation provides sustained container for working-through. Sequential relationship: CI surfaces, meditation integrates.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Sequential: CI provides awareness moments. M22 meditation provides integration mechanism. M16 applies: Protector permission required before assigning homework that touches difficult material. M9 applies: Ventral regulation prerequisite for safe home practice.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: When providing clinical guidance, integrate M21 environmental holding (consistent structure, emotional poise, survival without retaliation) WITH M22 internal stance (bare attention toward client's process, impartial/open/nonjudgmental). If clinician's own parts activate (impatience, need to fix, performance anxiety), this breaks both Winnicott holding and bare attention stance. Cannot provide holding while blended with own Manager.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered as ongoing clinician stance standard. Therapist's evenly suspended attention is itself a form of holding. Therapist must maintain this stance without memory or desire (not imposing agenda).
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Additive: M21 provides environmental structure (predictability, non-impingement); M22 adds that therapist's internal meditative stance is itself a form of holding. M16 applies: Clinician Self-Energy Prerequisite—cannot provide holding while blended with own parts.

phase6_rules.rule_name: Do Not Celebrate Progress When Basic Fault Active
phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: If basic fault / Hungry Ghost pattern is detected as active, then withhold celebration or progress framing in clinical output. Progress praise intensifies the basic fault wound by highlighting the gap between current state and idealized state. Use neutral acknowledgment instead of celebratory language.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when generating feedback for client with active basic fault presentation. The wound of absence is intensified by praise that implies client should feel better than they do.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: NOT IN SOURCE

phase6_rules.rule_name: Ventral State Confirms Bare Attention Capacity
phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: When using M9 state detection markers (spontaneous breath, prosody, eye contact) to assess client state, recognize that ventral markers confirm whether bare attention is actually present or whether a Manager is performing a bare-attention-like stance. If social engagement system is offline (no prosody, flat voice), bare attention cannot be authentically accessed—client may be in False Self compliance or Manager control.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when assessing whether client is genuinely accessing bare attention or performing it. Integration prevents mistaking intellectual understanding of bare attention for embodied capacity.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Confluent: M9 provides the autonomic mechanism that enables the attentional stance M22 describes. The seven qualities of bare attention are the phenomenological description of ventral vagal state. Both frameworks describe the same state from different angles: M9 from neurobiology, M22 from meditative phenomenology.

phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: When client shows ventral markers but cannot let go of control (Manager maintaining hypervigilance despite physiological safety), recognize that co-regulation establishes ventral state but surrender requires client's willingness to yield False Self's need for control. Meditation alone (creating ventral state) doesn't resolve basic fault—client must also develop surrender capacity, which requires psychotherapy to address the core wound that makes control feel necessary for survival.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when client shows ventral regulation but cannot release control. Distinguishes autonomic safety from psychological surrender.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Additive: M9's vagal brake creates physiological conditions (safety, reduced threat perception) that allow surrender, but surrender is the intentional ego capacity to not re-engage control when ventral state is present. M22 adds psychological mechanism (surrender) to M9's autonomic mechanism (vagal brake).

phase6_rules.rule_name: M9×M16 Feel Towards Gate Active in M22 Context
phase6_rules.mechanic_instruction: The Feel Towards gate from M16 remains active in M22 context. When assessing capacity for meditation or bare attention work, use Feel Towards test to confirm Self-energy presence. If answer to 'How do you feel toward that part/experience?' is not one of 8 Cs, a Concerned Part is blended and client cannot authentically access bare attention. Make the Concerned Part the target before proceeding with contemplative work.
phase6_rules.clinical_context: Triggered when assessing readiness for any M22 intervention. The M9×M16 conflict resolution (Feel Towards gate required before confirming ventral state) applies fully in M22 context.
phase6_rules.cross_module_integration: Modifier: M22 relies on M16's Feel Towards gate to distinguish genuine Self-energy/observing ego from Manager performing bare-attention-like behavior. Client could show ventral markers while capable Manager uses meditation as control strategy. Feel Towards test required before confirming bare attention capacity.

Phase5_DecisionTree.rule_name: Basic Fault Detection via Hungry Ghost Language
Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client uses language of 'emptiness,' 'unworthiness,' 'insatiable longing,' 'nothing fills me up,' or describes patterns of addiction, low self-esteem, and futile grasping to fill inner lack
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN flag as Hungry Ghost realm / basic fault activation. Withhold celebration or progress framing. Use neutral acknowledgment. Route to psychotherapy-first track. Do not deepen contemplative work. Basic fault must be addressed in relational holding before meditation is safe.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein explicitly warns that meditation can surface the basic fault without healing it. The Hungry Ghost pattern indicates inadequate childhood attention created a preverbal wound of absence. Client is searching for gratification for old needs whose time has passed. Meditation alone reinforces avoidance of this core wound.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Override only if client demonstrates stable Self-leadership (M16), wide Window of Tolerance (M9), and explicit therapy work on basic fault is running parallel. Otherwise default to psychotherapy-first.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M16: Check for Exile carrying unworthiness burden. M21: Assess for holding environment failure. M9: Confirm ventral regulation capacity before any meditation homework.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF intake reveals Western cultural background AND presents with feelings of alienation, emptiness, or basic fault indicators (Hungry Ghost markers) AND no established psychotherapy addressing core developmental wound
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN psychotherapy must come first or run parallel before introducing formal meditation practice. Use concentration techniques only within session for stabilization (Track 03 Grounding). Do not prescribe home meditation practice. Establish relational holding (M21) and address basic fault before deepening contemplative work.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein's cultural-psychological framework: Western estrangement pattern means client lacks the 'good enough' early attention that creates secure base. Eastern enmeshment pattern allows meditation-first. Western clients using meditation before psychotherapy creates clinical danger—surfaces wound without healing capacity.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Override if client demonstrates: (1) stable capacity for authentic relating without rigid True Self identity, (2) existing therapeutic relationship addressing basic fault, (3) explicit request for meditation with informed consent about risks.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M21: Confirm holding environment present. M16: Confirm Protectors trust Self-leadership before meditation homework. M9: Confirm ventral baseline before formal practice.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client reports extensive meditation practice AND continues to exhibit Hungry Ghost patterns (emptiness, insatiable longing, unworthiness) AND shows no behavioral change or improved relationships despite meditation
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN flag as defensive meditation / spiritual bypass. Client is using meditation to avoid basic fault rather than address it. Redirect to psychotherapy to address Exile/basic fault. Hold meditation practice at concentration level only. Use Feel Towards test (M16) to assess whether meditation has strengthened Manager or cultivated genuine Self-energy.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein explicitly warns meditation can be used defensively to bypass the basic fault. Manager can perform meditation as sophisticated control strategy to keep Exiles suppressed. True test: does practice lead to behavioral change and relational improvement, or just more sophisticated avoidance?
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Only proceed with meditation deepening if client demonstrates: (1) genuine curiosity toward emptiness (8 Cs, not judgment), (2) willingness to engage psychotherapy, (3) evidence that meditation is softening defenses not hardening them.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M16: Use Feel Towards test to distinguish genuine Self-energy from Manager performing Self. M18: Check if CI inquiry reveals meditation being used to avoid rather than explore difficult material.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF when prompted to observe internal sensation, client immediately analyzes ('I think it's because...'), judges ('This is bad'), or avoids (changes subject, fidgets) AND cannot sustain non-reactive awareness for even brief moments
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN client lacks bare attention capacity (observing ego not developed). Focus on cultivating observing ego through concentration practice (samadhi) within session only. Do not prescribe mindfulness (vipassana) practice. Use Stone 6 (M18) to anchor in present sensation but expect high difficulty. Strengthen observing ego before advancing meditation sequence.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein's three-stage progression requires concentration (samadhi) first to develop observing ego. Without this foundation, mindfulness and insight practices fail or become re-traumatizing. Client is 'swept away' by experience—needs capacity to witness before capacity to investigate.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: None. Observing ego capacity is prerequisite for all advanced work. Do not override this gate.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M16: Observing ego capacity parallels Self-energy capacity. If client cannot hold Self-presence, they cannot hold bare attention. M9: Confirm ventral state present—bare attention requires social engagement system online.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client reports meditation experiences of 'seeing dissolution,' 'feeling foundations crumble,' 'seeing lions/tigers/leopards,' or other imagery of threat/precariousness AND shows dorsal markers (flat voice, numb/foggy, disconnection) OR sympathetic FEAR markers (darting eyes, freezing, jumpiness)
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN immediately halt deepening of meditation practice. Client is experiencing Terror state at edge of Window of Tolerance. This is autonomic dysregulation, not spiritual breakthrough. Intervention is state restoration (Track 01 Settle) and co-regulation, not deeper practice. Client needs psychotherapy to address basic fault before meditation can safely continue.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein describes Terror states as 'seeing self's foundations as precarious.' M9 provides mechanism: client has been pushed to edge of Window of Tolerance where ventral regulation dissolves into sympathetic or dorsal activation. Without adequate holding, meditation surfaces dissolution that client's nervous system cannot tolerate.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Do not override. Terror states indicate meditation has exceeded client's capacity for safe ego deconstruction. Must restore regulation and address basic fault before proceeding.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M9: Terror state is sympathetic FEAR or dorsal collapse. Use NS state markers to confirm. M21: Lack of holding environment allows Terror state. Must establish relational safety.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client consistently uses spatial language to describe self: 'finding my core,' 'protecting my boundaries,' 'getting to the bottom of things,' 'I have layers,' 'discovering the real me'
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN client is in spatial self-metaphor stage (preliminary developmental stage). Parts work (M16) is appropriate. Temporal/processual language is premature—do not force it. Client needs structure and coherence at this stage. Use spatial language in interventions. Long-term goal is progression to temporal metaphor but only when client shows readiness.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein's developmental framework: spatial metaphor is preliminary stage where self is viewed as entity with boundaries, core, layers. This is not pathological—it's developmentally necessary for many Western clients with basic fault. Forcing temporal deconstruction prematurely destabilizes necessary achievements.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Only introduce temporal metaphor if client demonstrates: (1) stable Self-leadership, (2) most Exiles unburdened, (3) spontaneous curiosity about impermanence, (4) comfort with flux. Otherwise maintain spatial framework.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M16: Spatial metaphor aligns with parts model. This is appropriate for Phases 1-4. M21: Spatial coherence may be necessary when True Self is still emerging.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client spontaneously uses temporal language: 'I'm a process,' 'I'm always changing,' 'I'm learning to ride the waves,' 'I'm not the same person I was yesterday' AND shows comfort with impermanence AND demonstrates stable Self-leadership across states
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN client is ready for temporal self-metaphor framework. Can introduce mindfulness (vipassana) practice that observes flux. Can frame goals as processual rather than fixed-state achievements. Can begin questioning attachment to permanent identity. Phase 5-6 interventions appropriate.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein's framework: temporal metaphor represents developmental progression beyond spatial view. Self as process allows greater resilience and flexibility. This is prerequisite for advanced meditation (insight stage) where self-concept is deconstructed entirely.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Do not force temporal metaphor if client shows anxiety about impermanence, clings to fixed identity, or lacks stable Self-leadership. Spatial coherence may still be necessary.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M16: Temporal metaphor may conflict with parts model. Assess whether client can hold both: parts as useful map while recognizing processual nature. M9: Temporal metaphor requires wide Window of Tolerance.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client expresses 'I know why I do this but I can't stop' AND has intellectual insight into patterns ('remembering') but continues to enact them compulsively in life and therapeutic relationship ('repeating') AND shows frustration with lack of change despite therapy
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN client is stuck in repeating phase between remembering and working-through. Stop insight-focused work. Use Feel Towards test (M16) to identify concerned Protector blocking change. Get to know Protector's fears about change. Obtain explicit permission before returning to Exile/basic-fault work. Only when Protectors trust Self-leadership can working-through begin. Introduce mindfulness and surrender (M22) as explicit mechanism for working-through.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein: standard psychotherapy gets stuck when it achieves remembering but lacks mechanism for working-through. Repeating occurs when therapy touches Exile without Protector permission. Buddhist practices (mindfulness, surrender) provide the working-through mechanism Freud identified but lacked method for.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Do not override by providing more insight. More remembering will not resolve repeating phase. Must address Protector concerns and introduce embodied practice for integration.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M16: Repeating phase = Protector backlash. Use Protector negotiation protocol. M18: Use CI Stepping Stones to surface Protector concerns, especially Stone 4 ('What does it do for you?').

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client demonstrates: (1) ability to stay with difficult material using mindfulness instead of acting it out, (2) becoming more 'conversant with resistance' and changing view of it, (3) small but consistent behavioral changes integrating past insights, (4) uses awareness to notice and interrupt old patterns in moment
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN client is in working-through phase (Phases 3-5). Support and deepen use of mindfulness and surrender. Therapist role is providing holding environment for this long, repetitive integration process. Can advance meditation practice if autonomic capacity supports it. Continue tracking behavioral change as evidence of genuine integration vs. intellectual insight.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein: working-through is the long process of integrating unconscious material, becoming conversant with resistance. Client has moved beyond mere insight (remembering) and compulsive reenactment (repeating). This is the phase where Buddhist practices provide sustained container for integration.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Continue supporting working-through. Do not rush to advanced stages. Integration is inherently long and repetitive—therapist patience is key component.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M9: Working-through correlates with stable Green state. M16: Working-through = Protectors trust Self enough to allow change. M18: CI provides micro-interventions for working-through moments.

Phase5_DecisionTree.rule_name: Strong Ego Prerequisite for Insight Practice
Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client requests or is being considered for advanced meditation (insight/vipassana-nana stage that deconstructs self-concept) BUT does not demonstrate: (1) capacity to witness difficult material without blending (observing ego functional), (2) Protectors give permission without qualified yes (trust in Self established), (3) can tolerate intense emotion without Firefighter activation (strong ego resilient), (4) wide Window of Tolerance with capacity to traverse sympathetic and return to ventral safely
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN client lacks strong ego / Self-leadership prerequisite for advanced meditation. Do not proceed with insight practice. Continue building Self-leadership through concentration practice (samadhi) and Self-Protector relationship work (M16). Client needs more psychotherapy addressing basic fault before deconstructive meditation is safe. Without strong ego, deconstruction becomes fragmentation.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein explicitly requires 'strong ego' for advanced meditation, especially insight stage. Strong ego is NOT False Self control—it's observing ego resilient enough to witness dissolution without collapse. Identical to M16 Self-leadership: Self can hold space for Exiles without flooding, maintain 8 Cs under stress, earn Protector trust.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Do not override. This is safety-critical gate. Advanced meditation without strong ego causes Terror states or defensive bypass.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M16: Strong ego = Self-leadership capacity. Use Feel Towards test and Protector permission assessment. M9: Strong ego requires wide Window of Tolerance. M21: Strong ego requires secure holding environment history or therapeutic repair.

Phase5_DecisionTree.rule_name: Bare Attention Clinician Stance: High Client Reactivity
Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client shows high reactivity (intense emotional activation, rapid state shifts, flooding) AND verbal interventions increase dysregulation rather than support regulation
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN clinician shifts to explicit bare attention stance: maintain evenly suspended attention, withhold verbal intervention, model non-reactive presence. Clinician's own bare attention (impartial, open, nonjudgmental, interested, patient, fearless, impersonal) becomes the holding intervention. Can explicitly name the attentional quality being offered: 'I'm going to stay present with you without trying to fix or change what's happening.'
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein parallels bare attention with Freud's 'evenly suspended attention' and Winnicott's 'capacity to be alone in presence of another.' When client is flooded, clinician's non-reactive presence is more regulating than words. Modeling bare attention teaches client the stance through direct transmission.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Only override if client explicitly requests verbal support or if silence triggers abandonment terror. Otherwise maintain bare attention stance as primary intervention.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M9: Bare attention requires clinician in ventral state—cannot model this while blended with own Manager. M21: Bare attention IS a form of holding—emotional poise without impingement. M16: Clinician must be in Self-energy to hold bare attention stance.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client expresses goal to 'find my True Self' OR 'discover the real me' OR frames therapy as search for fixed authentic identity AND shows attachment to this as permanent state to achieve
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN introduce Epstein's caution: 'What if there's no fixed True Self to find, just authentic responding in each moment?' Reframe goal from finding thing (spatial) to cultivating capacity (temporal). Use M21 True Self work (recovering spontaneous gesture) in early phases, but do not reify True Self as object to possess. For Phase 6 clients with readiness indicators, can explicitly introduce Buddhist deconstruction: True Self concept itself is potential attachment.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein explicitly warns against reifying True Self as goal—treats it as another attachment, another spatial metaphor, another search for permanent state. M21 uses True Self as therapeutic endpoint; M22 warns this creates new trap. Must navigate tension carefully based on client developmental stage.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Early phases (1-4): default to M21 True Self language—client needs to distinguish spontaneous from compliant. Later phases (5-6): if client shows readiness, introduce temporal/processual reframe. Do not force Buddhist deconstruction prematurely.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M21: Direct conflict. M21 treats True Self as goal; M22 treats it as trap. Use developmental sequencing to resolve. M16: Parts model (spatial) may need reframing to processual view in later phases.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF considering prescribing meditation homework BUT client shows: (1) unstable ventral regulation (cannot maintain Green state without co-regulation), (2) no established relational holding (M21), (3) active Firefighter behaviors (addiction, self-harm, dissociation), (4) basic fault surfacing without therapy addressing it, OR (5) Protectors show qualified permission or concerns
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN do NOT prescribe meditation homework. Meditation becomes defensive bypass under these conditions. Use concentration techniques within session only for stabilization. Establish co-regulation capacity, relational holding, and address Firefighter concerns before assigning home practice. Client needs therapy-first or therapy-parallel before solo meditation.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein's clinical danger warning: meditation can surface basic fault without healing it. Without adequate holding and regulation capacity, home practice reinforces avoidance or causes overwhelm. Firefighter activation indicates Protectors don't trust client's capacity for safe self-regulation.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Only override if all five conditions are clearly absent and client has demonstrated stable capacity for self-regulation with meditation in session.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M9: Requires stable ventral baseline. M21: Requires holding environment established. M16: Requires Protector permission without qualified yes. M18: Can use brief Stone 6 embodiment in session as alternative to formal meditation.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client demonstrates: (1) stable Green/ventral state within session, (2) capacity for sustained focus on single object (breath, sensation) without flooding or dissociation, (3) observing ego showing seven qualities of bare attention (impartial, open, nonjudgmental, interested, patient, fearless, impersonal), (4) can witness thoughts/sensations without immediate reaction
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN client is ready for progression from concentration (samadhi) to mindfulness (vipassana) practice. Can introduce meditation that shifts attention across succession of objects to observe flux. Can begin temporal metaphor language. Continue tracking for Terror states or defensive use. Mindfulness stage requires resilient Window of Tolerance—must be able to observe flux without dysregulation.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein's three-stage progression: concentration creates tranquility and observing ego. Mindfulness observes flux and introduces temporal view. Cannot skip concentration stage—observing ego must be functional first. M9 provides autonomic map: concentration requires ventral regulation; mindfulness requires capacity to traverse sympathetic safely.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Do not advance to mindfulness if concentration capacity is unstable or if client shows dysregulation when observing changing sensations.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M9: Mindfulness requires wide Window of Tolerance, not just stable ventral. M16: Observing ego capacity parallels Self-leadership—must be able to witness parts without blending.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client intellectualizes, analyzes, or dissociates into story/narrative when discussing experience AND is disconnected from present embodied sensation
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN use Stone 6 (M18) as bridge to Epstein's 'remembering the present': 'That's analysis. What do you actually FEEL in your body right now?' Anchor client in present-moment sensation. This is bare attention enacted through CI language. If client cannot access present sensation (tuning out, dissociation), this indicates basic fault activation requiring co-regulation before inquiry can proceed.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein defines Buddhist remembering as returning attention to present-moment experience—third type of Freudian remembering focused on present therapeutic encounter. Stone 6 operationalizes this: healing happens in present embodied awareness, not narrative reconstruction. Both meditation and CI require embodied presence prerequisite.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: If Stone 6 causes flooding or shutdown, return to co-regulation (Track 01 Settle). Present-moment anchoring requires ventral state—cannot force it in Red/dorsal or unstable Amber/sympathetic.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M18: Stone 6 is the technique for present-moment remembering. M9: Present-moment awareness requires ventral state—social engagement system online. M21: Capacity for present-moment experience requires holding environment.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client presents Hungry Ghost pattern (insatiable longing, emptiness, unworthiness) AND inquiry surfaces that pattern originates in developmental moment where child learned to ignore gut/True Self to maintain caregiver relationship
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN use integrated M22/M18 framework for client education: 'You're not broken—you're searching for something that was missing early on' (M22 Hungry Ghost destigmatization). 'You learned to ignore what you needed to keep the relationship' (M18 attachment-wound mechanism). Use Stones 10-11 (imagine child, who would you want them to talk to) to access moment attachment was chosen over authenticity. Both frameworks agree: healing requires addressing developmental wound, not managing symptoms.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: M22 Hungry Ghost and M18 attachment-wound describe same developmental injury from complementary angles. Hungry Ghost is felt experience (insatiable longing); attachment wound is etiology (child sacrificed authenticity for connection). M18 adds that Hungry Ghost craving is broken Hallelujah—yearning from disconnection, searching externally for internal connection.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: None. This integration provides both phenomenological validation (M22) and developmental mechanism (M18) for comprehensive treatment.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M18: Stones 8-9 (origin tracing) and 10-11 (child visualization) directly access Hungry Ghost origin moment. M21: Hungry Ghost indicates holding environment failure. M16: Hungry Ghost pattern maintained by Exile carrying unworthiness burden.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF asked to observe part from Self-distance (M16 unblending) OR asked to hold bare attention toward internal experience, client shows: (1) inability to tolerate ambiguous intermediate zone where experience is neither 'me' nor 'not-me', (2) needs rigid boundaries, (3) cannot play with perspective, (4) shows False Self compliance when asked to observe
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN client lacks transitional space capacity—prerequisite for both meditation (M22) and parts work (M16). Focus on building holding environment (M21) and ventral regulation (M9) before proceeding with contemplative practice or deep parts exploration. Use concentration practice to develop observing capacity within secure relational container. Do not push unblending or bare attention until transitional space capacity emerges.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein applies Winnicott's transitional space to meditation: bare attention creates psychological space where self can be questioned without threat. M16 unblending occurs within this space—part is neither 'me' nor 'not-me.' M21 holding environment is prerequisite for developing this ambiguous intermediate capacity. Cannot force it prematurely.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Do not override. Transitional space capacity is foundational for both meditation and parts work. Without it, both interventions cause fragmentation or False Self compliance.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M21: Transitional space capacity is developmental achievement requiring adequate holding. M16: Unblending requires transitional space—cannot separate from part without capacity for intermediate zone. M9: Transitional space requires ventral state baseline.

Phase5_DecisionTree.condition: IF client has achieved insight via CI inquiry (Stone 13 identifies core belief, Stones 8-9 trace origin) BUT needs sustained mechanism for integrating awareness into behavioral change (stuck in repeating, not yet working-through)
Phase5_DecisionTree.then_action: THEN prescribe bare attention meditation as homework for working-through. Example: Stone 13 identifies belief 'I'm unworthy.' Meditation homework: notice when belief arises and apply bare attention without reaction. This extends CI beyond session—client develops observing ego through daily practice. Only prescribe if client has: (1) ventral regulation capacity, (2) basic relational holding established, (3) no active Firefighter behaviors. Otherwise meditation becomes defensive bypass.
Phase5_DecisionTree.rationale: Epstein's integration model: psychotherapy provides micro-interventions to surface material (CI Stones), meditation provides sustained container for working-through. CI's Stone 6 embodiment is not sufficient alone—sustained bare attention practice needed to integrate insights over time. Sequential relationship: CI surfaces, meditation integrates.
Phase5_DecisionTree.override_notes: Do not prescribe meditation homework if contraindications present (Rule M22_DT_13). Default to in-session Stone 6 embodiment as working-through tool if home practice unsafe.
Phase5_DecisionTree.cross_module_check: M18: CI provides awareness moments. M22 meditation provides integration mechanism. M16: Protector permission required before assigning homework that touches difficult material. M9: Ventral regulation prerequisite for safe home practice.
